statute: O.C.G.A. § 43-41-1 et seq.; Georgia Lien Law O.C.G.A. § 44-14-361
license_notes: License required for residential and commercial contracting in excess of $2,500. Unlicensed contracting over $2,500 makes the contract unenforceable and forfeits lien rights.

mandatory_clauses.label: Contractor license disclosure
Mechanics lien notice
mandatory_clauses.text:
NOTICE REGARDING LIENS

Under Georgia law, persons or companies who furnish labor, services, or materials for the improvement of your real property may have a claim against your property if they are not paid. This claim is known as a materialman's or mechanic's lien. If unpaid subcontractors, laborers, or suppliers file a lien against your property, you could be required to pay twice for the same work or materials. To protect yourself, obtain a lien waiver or release from each contractor, subcontractor, and material supplier before making final payment.

mandatory_clauses.label: Federal 3-day right to cancel (TILA)

penalties_for_non_compliance: Contracting without license over $2,500 = contract unenforceable, forfeiture of lien rights, civil penalties.
notes: Georgia requires a license for any residential or commercial contracting job over $2,500. Without a license, the contract is unenforceable and the contractor loses all lien rights. Federal 3-day notice satisfies state cancellation requirements. Georgia has reciprocity with Louisiana, Mississippi, North Carolina, and Tennessee for general contractors.
